flags: No step produced the parts — a cut step was inserted at index 0, enumerated from the cut list.

The 24 decorative 3" lag screws are named in the description and in the materials list but were used by no step; they are now called out at the notched leg joint, which is the joint the description says they dress.

Notch WIDTH and LOCATION are not stated in the source. The 5-1/2" width is DERIVED (it is the 2x6 stretcher's own width) and the 3/4" depth per leg board is the plan's own number — two boards each notched 3/4" capture the 1-1/2" stretcher. Where along the leg the notch falls is genuinely absent; the prose says to mark it off the assembled height rather than inventing a number.
Top-stud spacing is DERIVED, not authored: five studs across 88-1/2" aprons works out near 18" on center.

Length arithmetic does not fully close: the 96" top boards over 88-1/2" aprons leave 7-1/2", but a 3-1/2" leg at each end accounts for only 7". The prose says to keep the end overhangs even rather than asserting a figure.

The 2x2 shelf board supports are 81" long, but fifteen 5-1/2" shelf boards lay up 82-1/2" of run. The end boards are notched around the legs, so this may be intended; it is not resolvable from the data and the prose does not assert a fit.

Lumber verified with packBoards: 2x4 parts need 8 boards (8 supplied), 2x6 need 3 (3 supplied), 1x6 need 4 (4 supplied), 2x2 need 2 (2 supplied). No shortfall — but the 2x4 count is exact, with no spare board.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: Cut everything before you assemble anything, squaring every cut. From the eight 2x4s: four longer leg pieces at 34-1/2", four shorter leg pieces at 31", two top aprons at 88-1/2", five top studs at 20-3/4", two center legs at 31", and two inside center legs at 22". From the three 2x6s: two bottom shelf aprons at 88-1/2", two middle leg stretchers at 22-1/4", and two shorter bottom stretchers at 13-3/4". From the four 1x6s cut fifteen bottom shelf boards at 20-3/4", and from the two 2x2s cut two shelf board supports at 81". The three 2x10s are used at their full 8 ft length as the top — do not cut them. Allowing 1/8" for each saw kerf, all eight 2x4s get used, so cut the two 88-1/2" aprons first, one to its own board, and take the shorter parts out of what is left.

ops.title: Notch the leg pieces
ops.body: Each leg is two boards face to face — a 34-1/2" longer piece and a 31" shorter piece — and each gets a notch so the middle 2x6 stretcher passes through the pair rather than butting into it. Make the notch 5-1/2" wide to match the 2x6's width and 3/4" deep in each board, so the two 3/4" notches together form the 1-1/2" pocket the stretcher's thickness needs. Set the circular saw's blade depth to 3/4", make repeated kerf cuts across the notch area roughly every 1/4", then knock out and pare the waste with a chisel until the floor of the notch is flat and the stretcher slides in snugly. Clamp the two boards of a leg together and lay out both notches at once so they line up; a stretcher that meets two offset notches will twist the leg.

ops.title: Assemble the leg sets around the middle stretcher
ops.body: Slide a 22-1/4" middle stretcher into the notches of each leg set so it passes clean through, and clamp the two leg boards tight against it. Fasten with glue and 2-1/2" screws driven from the inside, starting in the longer 34-1/2" leg piece so the heads stay off the show face. Check the leg set for square before the glue grabs — a 13-3/4" shorter bottom stretcher laid in dry makes a good spacer to true it up. Drive the 3" lag screws through the show face into the stretcher at this joint if you want the exposed heads; they are decoration, so set them evenly rather than wherever they land.

ops.title: Add the top studs
ops.body: Stand the two 88-1/2" top aprons parallel and run the five 20-3/4" top studs between them, ends flush with the outside faces of the aprons so the frame comes out 20-3/4" deep. Space the studs evenly along the 88-1/2" run — that works out near 18" apart — and keep every stud square to the aprons, since these are what the tabletop lands on. Two 2-1/2" screws through the outside of each apron into each stud end is enough.

ops.title: Add the shelf board supports
ops.body: Screw the two 81" 2x2 supports to the inside faces of the 5-1/2" bottom shelf aprons, one front and one back, set down about 3/4" from the top edge so the 1x6 shelf boards drop in flush with the apron tops. Run them level with each other end to end — check with a straightedge rather than by eye, because any dip here shows up as a rocking shelf board.

ops.title: Lay the bottom shelf boards
ops.body: Lay the fifteen 20-3/4" 1x6 shelf boards across the supports, starting at the center and working outward to each end so any width discrepancy lands at the ends where it is least visible. The boards at each end have to be notched around the legs — hold each one in place, scribe the leg profile directly onto it, and cut to the line with a jigsaw rather than measuring, since the notch has to match the leg as built. Glue each board to the supports and set it with 1-1/4" finish nails.

ops.title: Attach the tabletop
ops.body: Lay the three full-length 9-1/4" wide 2x10s across the frame, edges tight together — that makes a top 27-3/4" deep over a 20-3/4" deep stud frame, so 3-1/2" of overhang to split evenly front and back. Center the 96" top on the base so the two end overhangs match, then glue and screw down into every stud and both top aprons with 2-1/2" screws. Work from the center board outward so the two outer boards can be nudged to keep the joints tight.

ops.title: Attach the casters and finish
ops.body: With the base fully assembled and checked square, tip the console over and screw a caster to the bottom of each leg, centered on the 3-1/2" leg face. Do this last, as the description says — a rolling base is far harder to true up than a stationary one. Sand every surface and ease the edges, then finish as desired.

baseStepTitles: Assemble the leg sets around the middle stretcher
Fill the void behind the center leg

flags: No step produced the parts — a cut step was inserted at index 0 from the cut list.

The cut list contains TWO different front aprons: a 'Front apron' (1x2, 14-1/2") and a 'Front and back aprons' pair (1x3, 14-1/2"). Which belongs to the seat base and which to the back frame is not determined by the data; the prose keeps them distinct by stock thickness and does not assert a role for the 1x3 pair beyond the base.
'Base back rail' is named in the singular but has quantity 2. Left as-is and not asserted in prose.
Step 'Build the base' calls for corner bracing (2x2 with both ends at 45°) that appears nowhere in the cut list, so no length is given for it.

The seat foam is 15-1/2" x 17-1/2", while the base aprons are 14-1/2" long and the side rails 13-1/2" — the foam is intentionally larger than the frame, which is normal for an upholstered seat but is not stated in the source. The prose does not claim a fit.
Lumber verified with packBoards: the 2x2 parts pack into exactly 2 boards, which is what the materials list supplies. 1x2, 1x3 and 2x3 each fit their single 8 ft board with room to spare.

step.title: Cut the frame parts
step.body: Every angled cut in this chair is 15°, so set the saw once and cut all of them together. From the two 8 ft 2x2s: two tall chair back sides at 18-1/8" and two short chair back sides at 15-3/8", each with one end at 15° and measured to the long point; one back support at 14-1/2"; one back base support at 13"; two front legs at 16" square on both ends; and two base back rails at 16-9/16" with both ends at 15° and parallel to each other. From the 1x3, cut two front and back aprons at 14-1/2". From the 1x2, cut the chair back cross piece at 16-3/16" with one end at 15° to the long point, and the front apron at 14-1/2". From the 2x3, cut two base side rails at 13-1/2", each with one end at 15° off square measured to the SHORT point — note that these are short-point measurements while the back pieces are long-point, so read the cut list before each cut rather than working from memory. Both 2x2s are fully used, so lay the four back-side pieces out first.

ops.title: Build the chair back
ops.body: Lay the two 18-1/8" tall back sides flat and parallel with their 15° ends matching, and set the 16-3/16" 1x2 cross piece between them, against their inside faces, so it spans 16-3/16" clear. Glue it and drive 2" screws through the outside of each back side into the cross piece, then add pocket hole screws and a screw from the back into the same joint. That triple fastening is deliberate — the back legs here are straight rather than curved, so this corner carries the load a curved leg normally would, and one line of screws is not enough. Check the frame for square before the glue sets; a racked back will not sit flat against the base.

ops.title: Attach the front and top
ops.body: Add the 14-1/2" 1x2 front apron across the front of the frame and the chair back cross piece across the top, keeping both square to the uprights. Pocket hole screws work here, or 2" screws into the thin front apron and 3" screws into the top where you are driving into 2x2 stock. These joints get reinforced by later parts, so they do not have to be fully structural on their own — but they do have to be square, because everything after this registers off them.

ops.title: Attach the back base support
ops.body: Fit the 13" back base support across the back of the frame, low down where it ties the back assembly into the seat base — this is the part that keeps the back from flexing rearward. Fasten it with pocket holes, or with 3" screws driven through the uprights into its ends, and glue either way.

ops.title: Build the base
ops.body: The base is the two 16" front legs, the two 13-1/2" 2x3 base side rails with their 15° ends toward the back, the two 14-1/2" 1x3 aprons front and back, and the 16-9/16" base back rails. If you do not have 2x3 on hand, double up two 1x3s face to face to make the side rails. When pocket-holing from the inside into the 1-1/2" square legs, set the jig as if for 3/4" stock and use 1-1/4" pocket hole screws — a 2-1/2" screw driven into the narrow face of a 2x2 leg will split it. Corner bracing cut from 2x2 offcuts, both ends at 45° and perpendicular to each other, adds real strength in the seat corners; glue every joint, since glue is what keeps a chair frame from working loose over years of racking.

baseStepTitles: Cut and sew the seat cover

flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the 'Legs' (4 at 1-1/2" x 1-1/2" x 35-1/4") and 'Front trim / legs' (2 at 1-1/2" x 1-1/2" x 62-1/4") call for 1-1/2" square stock, and NO 2x2 or other 1-1/2"-thick material appears in the materials list. The data does not determine which it should be, so the inserted cut step names the gap and offers laminating two 1x2s face to face rather than inventing a board.
The plywood steps existed but no step produced the solid-pine parts — a pine cut step was inserted at index 3.
'Pine, 1x3, 6 ft' (quantity 2) matches no cut-list part. It is most likely the applied-trim alternative described in the center-divider step, but that is not stated.

Drawer-box geometry taken from scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs and not re-derived: the 14" sides run the full depth and the 28-3/4" front/backs sit BETWEEN them, giving a 29-3/4" outside width, which is exactly the 29-3/4" drawer bottom.

Lumber verified with packBoards: the 1x2 parts need 5 boards and 5 are supplied (four 8 ft plus one 6 ft) — but only if the 67-1/4" top trim goes on the 6 ft board, which is the tightest constraint in the plan. The 1x8 parts need 2 of the 3 supplied; the 1x5 parts need 1 of 1.

ops.title: Cut drawer bottoms
ops.body: Cut eight drawer bottoms from 1/4" plywood at 14" x 29-3/4" — the same size serves the six large and two small boxes, since only the box height differs between them. That 29-3/4" is the outside width of a finished box: 28-3/4" front/back captured between two 1/2" sides. You may not need a full second 1/4" sheet if you have usable scrap on hand.

step.title: Cut the solid-pine trim and drawer fronts
step.body: Cut the pine before assembly starts, because the face-frame parts have to be on hand as the carcass goes together. From the 1x2 stock (1-1/2" wide as milled): six drawer dividers at 30-3/4", four side trim at 14-1/4", one back brace at 62-1/4", one front top trim at 67-1/4", and two top trim sides at 18-1/4". Allowing 1/8" a kerf those fill all five 1x2 boards, and the fit only works if the 67-1/4" top trim comes off the 6 ft board — cut that piece first. From the 1x8, whose 7-1/4" width is already the finished drawer-front height, crosscut six large drawer fronts at 30-1/2"; from the 1x5, whose 4-1/2" width is likewise the finished height, cut two small drawer fronts at 30-1/2". No ripping is needed for any drawer front. The four 35-1/4" legs and two 62-1/4" front trim/leg pieces are 1-1/2" square, and no 1-1/2"-thick stock is listed in the materials — either buy 2x2 for them or laminate two lengths of 1x2 face to face with glue and clamps for each one before cutting to length.

ops.title: Build the drawer boxes
ops.body: Build six large boxes from the 5-3/4" wide parts and two small boxes from the 3-1/2" wide parts, all from 1/2" plywood. In every box the two 14" sides run the full depth and the 28-3/4" front and back sit between them, which makes the finished box 29-3/4" wide overall — check that against the 29-3/4" bottoms you already cut before you screw a second box together. Keep the row labels from the ripping step paired: a front and back from the same row, sides from the same row. Put pocket holes at the front and at the very back of each box — the drawer front hides the front holes and nobody sees the back ones. Check each box for square across both diagonals as you clamp it.

baseStepTitles: Rip and label all the drawer parts
Attach the legs to the sides
Cut the center divider's insert openings
Attach the back and add hardware

flags: scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan, so the carcass reading was derived from the plan's own numbers instead and is stated with its proof: the 16" shelf and supports sit BETWEEN the two 3/4" side panels, giving 16" + 3/4" + 3/4" = 17-1/2", which is exactly the listed back-panel width and 1/4" per side narrower than the 18" face frame. If that is wrong, the back panel and the face frame are both wrong with it.

CORRECTNESS DEFECT in the face-frame cut list: 'Face frame top/mid rails' are 18" (quantity 2) and 'Face frame bottom rail' is 15" (quantity 2). An 18" rail spans the full frame width with the stiles between it, while a 15" rail fits between two 1-1/2" stiles — the two readings cannot both be right, and six pieces are listed where a three-drawer frame needs two stiles and up to four rails. The data does not settle it, so the rewritten step tells the builder to pick one convention and cut all four rails to it, and does not assert which.

Overall cabinet height is not resolvable: the side panels are 34-1/2", the back is 32", and the face frame stiles are 27", but the toe-kick notch depth is never given. The prose says to cut the notch from a pattern block, as the source does, and asserts no dimension.
One 4x8 sheet of 3/4" plywood covers every 3/4" part with room to spare — checked by area (about 2,163 sq in of parts against 4,608 sq in of sheet) and by a rip layout that fits.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: Break the 3/4" sheet down before anything else. Rip two 22-1/4" strips off the 48" width — that width sets a finished 24" cabinet depth once the face frame and drawer faces are on, and every cabinet in a run has to match it. From the first strip crosscut the two side panels at 34-1/2" and the bottom shelf at 16"; from the second strip rip a 5" band for the two bottom supports at 16", and take the two 3-1/2" top supports at 16" from the 3-1/2" strip left over after the two 22-1/4" rips. Cut the 1/4" back to 17-1/2" x 32". From the 1x2, cut two face frame stiles at 27" and four rails to your chosen frame layout (see the face-frame step). From the 3/4" face material cut one small drawer face at 5-1/2" x 17-1/2" and two large ones at 11" x 17-1/2". A single 4x8 sheet covers all the 3/4" plywood parts with plenty left over.
materials: Drawer/door face material, 3/4" plywood or MDF

ops.title: Cut the toe kick and drill the side panels
ops.body: Cut the toe kick notch into the bottom front corner of each 22-1/4" x 34-1/2" side panel with a jigsaw; make a pattern block from scrap and trace it onto both panels so the two notches are identical, which matters because the cabinet stands on them. Then drill 3/4" pocket holes along the OUTSIDE face of both panels, in from each long edge where the bottom shelf, base supports and top supports will land. Lay the two panels side by side, mark which is left and which is right, and confirm they are true mirror images before the drill touches either — the pocket holes go on the outside of both so the face frame can hide them, and it is genuinely easy to drill the second panel as a duplicate instead of a mirror.

ops.title: Attach the base supports
ops.body: Lay one side panel flat, pocket holes facing up and away from you, and stand the two 5" x 16" base supports on their edges across it — one flush with the front edge above the toe kick, one at the back edge. Because they are 16" long and land between the two 3/4" panels, the carcass will finish 17-1/2" wide overall. Screw through the side panel's pocket holes into each support with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, keeping the supports square to the panel edge.

ops.title: Attach the bottom shelf
ops.body: Set the 22-1/4" x 16" bottom shelf on top of the two base supports, its front edge flush with the front support and its side edge tight to the side panel. Screw down into both supports and across into the side panel with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws. The 16" shelf and the 16" supports are the same length on purpose — they set the cabinet's inside clear width, so if the shelf ends up proud of a support, fix it now rather than after the second side goes on.

ops.title: Close the carcass
ops.body: Lower the second side panel onto the assembly, pocket holes again facing outward, and drive 1-1/4" pocket hole screws into the ends of both base supports and the bottom shelf. Add the two 3-1/2" x 16" top supports across the top, one at the front and one at the back, which is what holds the box to width up there and gives the countertop something to screw into later. Measure both diagonals across the open front before the last screws go in — 17-1/2" of clear box width at the bottom and something different at the top means the drawers will bind.

ops.title: Build and attach the face frame
ops.body: Build the face frame flat on a table before it goes anywhere near the cabinet, joining the two 27" stiles to the rails with 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" screws and glue, and checking both diagonals as you clamp. The cut list is inconsistent about the rails — two are listed at 18" (full frame width, stiles between) and two at 15" (fitting between two 1-1/2" stiles) — so pick one convention and cut all four rails to it before you assemble; mixing the two gives you a frame that is neither 18" wide nor square. Size the upper drawer opening slightly larger than the lower one on purpose: that is what lets the two large drawer faces come out identical with the rail centered between them, and it is not an error to correct. Attach the finished frame to the cabinet front with pocket holes and glue, leaving about 1/4" of frame proud on each side of the 17-1/2" box.

ops.title: Build and install the drawers
ops.body: Measure the actual clear width inside your cabinet — it should be about 16" between the side panels — and build each drawer box narrower by the clearance your slides require, which for typical side-mount slides is about 1/2" per side, so roughly 15" wide. Build the boxes deep enough to suit the slides listed, whose 21-1/2" to 22-1/4" interior length matches the 22-1/4" carcass depth. Mount the slides level and at matching heights on both sides of each opening, measuring up from the bottom shelf rather than down from the top, then hang the boxes and check that each rolls without rubbing the face frame.
materials: Drawer slide sets, 21-1/2" to 22-1/4" interior length

ops.title: Attach the drawer faces
ops.body: The faces are full overlay at 17-1/2" wide — a 1/4" reveal against the 18" face frame on each side — with the small face 5-1/2" tall at the top and the two large faces 11" each below it. Working one drawer at a time, shim the face into position with 1/8" spacers, clamp it to the drawer box, and drive screws from INSIDE the box through the box front into the face so no fastener shows. Do the top face first and work down, checking the reveals with the same spacer each time rather than by eye.
materials: Drawer/door face material, 3/4" plywood or MDF

baseStepTitles: Rip the plywood and cut the toe kick
Build and attach the face frame

flags: SHORTFALL, verified with packBoards: the 1x2 parts (two front trim rails at 75-1/2", two center sides at 22-3/8", two center shorts at 5-5/8") need THREE 8 ft boards and the materials list supplies two. Each 75-1/2" rail consumes its own board and leaves under 21", which will not yield a 22-3/8" piece. Called out in the inserted cut step.

CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the baseboard rows ('Baseboard, front' and 'Baseboard, sides') list their material as 1x4, but the materials list carries 'Base moulding, 4-1/4 in, 10 ft' and the baseboard step calls it moulding. Cutting them from 1x4 would push the 1x4 requirement past the seven boards supplied; taking them from the moulding leaves the 1x4 count correct at exactly seven. The prose treats them as moulding and says so.

'Drawer box sides' has quantity 2 for a three-drawer cabinet, while 'Drawer box front/back' has quantity 6. Six sides are needed. The prose says to cut sides to suit your own openings and does not repeat the 2.

The three 24" drawer faces come out of one 6 ft 1x6 with no allowance for saw kerf (24 x 3 = 72" exactly). They are marked 'cut to fit each drawer opening' anyway, so the prose says to cut them last and expect the third to be trimmed.

packBoards checks that PASS: the 2x4 parts pack into exactly 6 boards (6 supplied, under 1" of slack on the fullest board); the 1x4 parts excluding baseboards pack into 7 (7 supplied); the two 2x6 top boards take one board each; one 3/4" plywood sheet and one 1/4" sheet cover their parts.

scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan, so no claim is made about which drawer-box member runs full length; the drawers are built to the real openings, as the source intends.

step.title: Cut the parts
step.body: From the six 2x4s: four legs at 34-1/2", four panel end caps at 10-1/2", two bottom supports at 75-1/2", two back framing rails at 77", and two back cleats at 18". All six boards are used and the fit is tight, so cut the two 77" rails and the two 75-1/2" supports first, one to a board. From the two 2x6s cut one 77" top board each. From the seven 1x4s: six side panels at 23-1/2", eight door stiles at 22-1/4", eight door rails and center panel frames at 11", two center divider trim at 18-1/8", six drawer box front/backs at 15-1/2", and drawer box sides at 16-3/4" — cut six of those, one pair per drawer, not the two the cut list shows. From the 1x2 cut two front trim rails at 75-1/2", two center sides at 22-3/8" and two center shorts at 5-5/8": buy THREE 8 ft 1x2s rather than the two listed, because each 75-1/2" rail eats a whole board and leaves under 21", which will not yield a 22-3/8" piece. Rip the 3/4" plywood into 16-3/4" strips for the 75-1/2" bottom shelf and the two 30-1/4" cabinet bay floors, and cut the 1/4" back panel 32" x 77". Leave the drawer faces and the baseboard until their own steps — both are cut to fit.

ops.title: Build the end panels
ops.body: Each end is its own small frame-and-panel assembly and it goes together inside-out: start by joining three 23-1/2" 1x4 side panels edge to edge, then cap that panel top and bottom with the 10-1/2" 2x4 end caps, then add a 34-1/2" leg to each side. Use 1-1/2" pocket holes and 2-1/2" pocket hole screws, all placed on the inside faces so nothing shows on the finished ends. Keep every back edge dead flush as you work, but leave the side panel deliberately inset from the outer face of the legs — that recess is the frame-and-panel look, and if you flush it up instead the end reads as a slab. Build both ends and set them side by side to confirm they match before moving on.

ops.title: Build the storage interior
ops.body: The 75-1/2" plywood bottom shelf runs the full span between the ends and sits on the two bottom supports; the two 30-1/4" bay floors then divide the case into a center drawer stack flanked by a door cabinet at each end. Set the two 18-1/8" center divider trim pieces into the base flush to the back, square to the shelf, and check them with a level and a square before anything gets fastened — the front trim in the next step has to land flush to the inside faces of the outer cabinets, and it cannot do that if the dividers are out. Glue and pocket-screw every joint, and measure the two door openings and the center opening once the dividers are set, because the doors and drawers are built to those real numbers.

ops.title: Add the front trim
ops.body: Run the two 75-1/2" 1x2 rails across the top and bottom of the case front, then fit the two 22-3/8" center sides and the two 5-5/8" center shorts around the center drawer bay. Keep everything square and straight — this trim is what the drawer faces will be gapped against, so a bowed rail shows up three times over. The center trim sits flush to the inside faces of the two side cabinets while overhanging the center bay evenly on each side. Glue and 1-1/4" brads, and clamp rather than relying on nail pressure to pull a bow out.

ops.title: Build and install the drawers
ops.body: Measure each of the three center-bay openings for real rather than working from the cut list, then build each box to the opening less the slide clearance — most side-mount slides want about 1/2" per side, so a box roughly 1" narrower than its opening. Use the 15-1/2" front/back pieces with 16-3/4" sides, and check each box across both diagonals. Mount the 15" slides set back 3/4" from the front of the case so there is room for the drawer face to sit flush with the trim. Hang the boxes, then cut each 5-1/2" wide face to fit its own opening from the 1x6 — cut them last and expect to trim the third, since three 24" faces use the full 6 ft board with nothing left for saw kerf. Fix the faces from inside the box with glue and 1-1/4" brads once you have shimmed an even gap all round.

ops.title: Add the baseboard
ops.body: Cut the base moulding to fit the bottom of the case, mitering the outside corners at 45° so the profile wraps continuously — measure each run off the assembled cabinet rather than off the cut list, since the finished width depends on how your end panels came out. Dry-fit the whole run before nailing anything: a mitered corner that does not close cannot be fixed after the glue is in. Glue the miters and set the moulding with brad nails.

flags: No step produced the parts — a cut step was inserted at index 0, carrying the source's own warning that the long-span dimensions are that builder's room and must be re-measured.

Drawer-box geometry taken from scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs and not re-derived: the 24" drawer sides run the full depth and the 22" front/backs sit BETWEEN them (23-1/2" outside, matching the 23-1/2" bottom); the third drawer's 22-1/2" front/backs give a 24" outside width, matching its 24" bottom.
The shoe tilt-out storage is built from '1x3 scrap' per the source, and no 1x3 appears in the materials list or cut list — no dimension is asserted for it.
'Loft floor decking' is a single cut-list row at 96" with the note that quantity depends on your loft footprint; the prose repeats that rather than implying one board does the floor.
Bed-frame arithmetic is not fully resolvable: the side rails and front rail are 80" and the joists 58-1/2", but no row states the finished inside width, so the prose does not claim a mattress size.

Lumber verified with packBoards: the 2x4 parts pack into exactly 14 boards and 14 are supplied — no spare, and the 94-1/2" room-length cleat needs a full 8 ft board on its own. The four 58-1/2" bed joists take exactly the two 10 ft 2x8s; the 1x6 drawer parts need 3 boards and 3 are supplied.

step.title: Measure your room, then cut the parts
step.body: Read every long-span number here as a worked example from the source's own room, not a cut-and-go dimension: the room-length wall cleat is listed at 94-1/2" because that was their room's width, and the 52" and 71" wall posts were cut to their ceiling and loft heights. Measure your own wall-to-wall span and ceiling height first and substitute. Then cut: from the two 6 ft 4x4s, one short wall post at 52" and one tall at 71". From the fourteen 2x4s: four wall studs at 53", two short ladder supports at 13", one ladder support at 8", two bed side rails at 80", four bed legs at 16-1/2", wall cleats at 76", 80" and your room length, one short loft joist at 60", and four loft joists at 82". Those fill all fourteen boards with nothing to spare, and the room-length cleat needs a full 8 ft board to itself, so cut the long pieces before the short ones. From the 2x8s: the 80" bed front rail off the 8 ft board, and four 58-1/2" bed joists off the two 10 ft boards, two per board. From the three 1x6s, six drawer sides at 24", four drawer front/backs at 22" and two more at 22-1/2" for the third drawer. Cut the plywood drawer bottoms at 24" x 23-1/2" (two) and 24" x 24" (one), and leave the drawer and door faces until the openings exist.
materials: 1/4" plywood, full sheet (drawer bottoms, shoe storage backs)

ops.title: Build the wall system
ops.body: Build the 4x4 wall frame first — the short 52" post, the tall 71" post, and the four 53" 2x4 studs between them — since everything else in this system hangs off it. Drill the pocket holes so they land on the UNDERSIDE of the horizontal members where they enter the posts, which keeps every hole out of sight once the wall backing goes on. Use 2-1/2" pocket hole screws and glue, and check the frame for square and plumb against the actual wall before it is fastened, not after.

ops.title: Build the bed support and attach to the wall
ops.body: Assemble the 2x4 bed support from the two 80" side rails and the four 16-1/2" legs, and take real trouble over square here — this frame becomes the cavity the drawers run in, and an out-of-square opening is what makes a drawer bind on its slides halfway out. Fasten the frame to itself and into the room's wall studs with 2-3/4" self-tapping screws, and into the 4x4 posts with the 5" lag screws, predrilling every lag so the post does not split. Level the frame in both directions before the last fasteners go in.

ops.title: Build the bed joists
ops.body: Frame the bed platform from the 2x8s — the 80" front rail and the four 58-1/2" joists — using 2-3/4" self-tapping screws through the rail into each joist end. The 7-1/4" depth of a 2x8 is a deliberate choice: it is what leaves a tall enough cavity underneath for the drawers. If you are skipping the drawers, 2x4 or 2x6 framing carries the same load and costs less. Space the joists evenly across the 80" rail and check the frame is square and flat before it takes any weight.

ops.title: Attach the wall cleats
ops.body: The loft floor rests on these, so they carry real load: find the wall studs, mark them, and screw each cleat into the framing with 2-3/4" screws — never into drywall alone. Set each cleat with a level rather than by measuring down from a ceiling, since a ceiling is rarely level and a loft floor that follows one is worse than one that does not. Cut the longest cleat to your own room's measured length, not to the 94-1/2" in the cut list.

ops.title: Lay the loft floor
ops.body: Frame the loft floor from the four 82" joists and the 60" short joist, resting on the wall cleats you just set, then deck it in 2x6 tongue and groove. The thicker T&G is not a style choice — the floor crosses a doorway opening close to 36" wide with no support beneath it, and 1x material is explicitly not strong enough over that gap. Start each course tongue-out, tap the next board home with a scrap offcut and a hammer so you never strike the tongue itself, and blind-nail through the tongue with 1-1/4" finish nails so the fasteners disappear. Buy enough T&G for your loft's actual footprint; the single 96" row in the cut list is one board, not the whole floor.
materials: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling

ops.title: Build the drawers and shoe storage
ops.body: Measure each opening under the bed platform before cutting anything, and build each box to that opening less the clearance the 24" bottom-mount slides require. In each box the two 24" sides run the full depth and the front and back sit between them: the 22" front/backs give a 23-1/2" outside width matching the 23-1/2" bottom, and the third drawer's 22-1/2" front/backs give 24", matching its 24" bottom. Check that against your own opening before you assemble the second box. Glue and nail the 1/4" plywood bottoms on with 1-1/4" finish nails, checking each box square as the bottom goes on, since the bottom is what locks the box square. Box out the tilt-out shoe storage from 1x3 scrap with a plywood back, sized to its own opening.
materials: 1/4" plywood, full sheet (drawer bottoms, shoe storage backs)

ops.title: Attach the drawer and door faces
ops.body: Hang the drawer boxes on their slides and the doors on their hinges first, then fit the faces to what you actually have. The two 7" x 24-1/4" drawer faces, the 7" x 24-3/4" third face, the two 16-1/4" x 24-1/4" door panels and the 16-1/4" x 24-3/4" third panel are all cut from the 3/4" plywood. Shim each face with 1/8" spacers on all four sides, clamp it, then screw it from INSIDE the drawer box so no fastener shows on the front. Work across the run checking the gaps with the same spacer every time — an even 1/8" all round is what makes a built-in read as built-in.
materials: Bottom side mount drawer slides, 24 in, pair

ops.title: Add the bed slats and hidden storage door
ops.body: Lay the eight 80" slats across the bed joists and fasten them down, splicing any joint directly over a joist so no slat end is unsupported. Then hinge the 52-3/4" x 12-3/4" plywood hidden storage door over the front opening. Cut a finger pull into it rather than fitting a knob — a knob on a door at shin height in a tiny house is something you will kick. Check the door swings clear of the drawer faces below it before the hinges are final.
materials: 2x6 tongue and groove, for loft floor/ceiling

ops.title: Add the ladder and railing
ops.body: Assemble the black iron pipe into a ladder and railing and anchor it into the 2x4 ladder supports and the loft framing, not into paneling — the two 13" short supports and the 8" support exist to give those anchors something solid to land in. Predrill every fastener into the framing. Understand the tradeoff the source names openly: iron pipe is not code-approved guardrail hardware, so treat this as a fixture you have judged for your own household rather than a certified rail, and test it under load before anyone climbs it.
materials: Black iron pipe, for ladder and railing

baseStepTitles: Build the bed support and attach to the wall
Build the drawers and shoe storage
Attach the drawer and door faces
Add the bed slats and hidden storage door

flags: CORRECTNESS DEFECT: the only cutting tool listed is a table saw, and the plan's whole premise is a leaf or curved outline. A table saw cannot cut a curve. The rewritten step says to keep the outline to straight cuts with the listed tools, or add a jigsaw or band saw — it does not silently assume a tool the plan does not list.

The dowels are all sold at their finished lengths (four 32" legs, two 18" short braces, one 32" long brace), so the original 'cut the dowels to length' step is a no-op unless you deliberately shorten the legs. The rewrite says so rather than sending someone to a saw for nothing.
The leg splay ANGLE is not given anywhere in the source. The prose describes setting the angled drill attachment and testing on scrap, and asserts no degree figure.

Brace placement is not specified either — the cut list gives two 18" short braces and one 32" long brace but no height up the legs. The prose says to fit them to the legs as splayed and does not invent a height.
The 1-1/4" leg dowels are drilled into a 3/4" panel, so the leg holes are through-holes by arithmetic — the panel is thinner than the dowel is wide. That is stated; no depth is invented.
This plan is published: false. Rewritten anyway so it is buildable if it is ever published.

ops.title: Cut the panel to shape
ops.body: Start from a 3/4" oak plywood blank 16" x 24", then lay your leaf or geometric outline onto it with a paper template so both halves match and you can move the shape around before committing. Keep the outline to straight cuts if the table saw is the only saw you have — a table saw cannot cut a curve, and the joinery in this project is what makes it work, not the outline. If you want a true leaf shape, cut it with a jigsaw or band saw and clean the edge with sandpaper wrapped around a block. Whatever shape you choose, leave enough solid material at the four leg positions to take a 1-1/4" hole without breaking out at an edge.

ops.title: Drill the angled leg holes
ops.body: Mark the four leg positions on the panel and drill each with a 1-1/4" forstner bit to match the leg dowels. Set an angled drill attachment so the bit leans out consistently — the splay is what gives this piece its stance, and four legs at four slightly different angles will rock. Test the setup on an offcut of the same 3/4" plywood first, and check that the sample dowel stands the way you want before you touch the real panel. Because the panel is only 3/4" thick and the holes are 1-1/4" across, these come out as through-holes; back the panel with scrap so the bit does not blow out the underside, and clamp the panel down rather than holding it.

ops.title: Check the dowel lengths
ops.body: The dowels are sold at their finished lengths, so there is usually nothing to cut: the four 1-1/4" leg dowels come at 32", the two 1" short braces at 18", and the 3/4" long brace at 32". Because the legs splay, the stand will sit lower than 32" tall and the feet will need to be trimmed square to the floor once the legs are in — stand the assembly up on a flat surface, scribe a line around each foot parallel to the floor, and cut to it. Do that after assembly, not before, since the trim depends on the splay you actually drilled.

ops.title: Assemble
ops.body: Glue the four leg dowels into their angled holes, seating each one fully and wiping the squeeze-out before it dries — end-grain-to-plywood glue joints are stronger than they look, but only if the dowel is fully home. With the legs set, hold the two 18" short braces across each pair of legs and the 32" long brace between the pairs, mark them where they actually land against the splayed legs, and fasten with pocket screws and glue at each contact point. Bracing a splayed set of legs is what stops the stand from racking sideways under a loaded pot, so fit each brace tight rather than close.

ops.title: Sand and finish
ops.body: Sand the panel faces and the sawn outline smooth, and pay particular attention to the plywood edge, which is the one surface that shows the piece is plywood — sand it fine and it takes finish far better. Ease the sharp corners of the outline and the cut ends of the legs. Finish as desired; oak plywood takes a clear finish well, and a stand meant to carry a potted plant is worth sealing on the top face against water.

flags: SHORTFALL, verified with packBoards: the 1x3 parts (two front decorative trim at 16-1/4" and one seat back trim at 36") need TWO 4 ft boards and the materials list supplies one. The plan's own cut step already tallies 'two 4 ft Pine 1x3' — the materials list is what is wrong. Called out in the affected step.

The 8 ft 1x2 is exactly consumed by the 35" back top rail, two 12" drawer slides and the 36" seat trim: 95" of parts plus kerf in a 96" board, with 5/8" to spare. Buy a second if your saw kerf is wide or a cut goes wrong.

Drawer-box geometry taken from scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs and not re-derived: the 11-1/2" drawer sides run the full depth and the 34-1/4" front and back sit BETWEEN them, giving a 35-3/4" outside width — exactly the listed drawer bottom and drawer face.

The bench's overall length is not resolvable from the data: the front apron, back supports and seat panel are all 36" while the back panel is 39" long, and nothing states whether the 36" members run outside the end panels or between them. The prose describes the joints without asserting which member is captured.

The 'End decorative caps', 'End decorative trim' and 'Front decorative trim' are all listed as rectangles with no curve profile, yet the jigsaw is listed for a 'decorative curved end profile' and the description calls the design decorative. No curve dimension exists, so the prose says to draw your own profile and cut to it.
'Leg braces' (two at 5-1/4") have no stated location. Not asserted.

ops.title: Build the two ends
ops.body: Each end is an 11-1/4" x 32-1/4" 1x12 panel that will get two 36" legs, a decorative trim piece and a cap. Before assembling anything, drill 3/4" pocket holes down BOTH long edges of each end panel so the legs can be pulled in from behind in the next step. The critical move is at the layout table: set the two panels side by side and mark one 'left, inside face' and the other 'right, inside face', because the two ends are mirror images and not duplicates. If you drill both panels with the pocket holes facing the same way, one end will show its holes on the finished outside of the bench, and there is no fixing that after assembly.

ops.title: Add the decorative front piece
ops.body: The 16-1/4" 1x3 front decorative trim and the 11-1/4" 1x6 end decorative trim dress the front edge of each end assembly. The cut list gives these as plain rectangles, so draw whatever curved profile you want onto one of them, cut it with the jigsaw, then use that finished piece as a template to trace the second — tracing is what makes the two ends match, since no two freehand curves ever will. Sand the sawn curve fair before it goes on. Remember these are mirrored: the profile on the left end has to face the same way relative to the front of the bench as the one on the right, which means the two pieces are cut as opposites, not as a pair. Note that the two 16-1/4" trim pieces plus the 36" seat back trim will not come out of a single 4 ft 1x3 — you need two of those boards, as the cut step's own tally says.

ops.title: Add the drawer slides
ops.body: The drawer rides on wood, not hardware: screw the two 12" 1x2 slides to the inside of each end assembly, level with each other and level front to back. Measure both from the same reference — the top of the front apron is the easiest — rather than from the floor, since the legs may not be perfectly equal. Check with a straightedge laid across both slides before the second one is fastened; two slides even 1/16" apart in height will make the drawer rock and rub as it opens. Wax the top face of each slide once they are in.

ops.title: Build the drawer box
ops.body: The box is two 11-1/2" sides running the full depth with the 34-1/4" front and back captured between them, which makes it 35-3/4" wide overall — the same as the 35-3/4" x 11-1/2" plywood bottom and the 35-3/4" drawer face. Before you cut, measure the actual opening in your bench and confirm you have clearance: wood slides need a little slop to run at all, so if the box comes out the same width as the opening, trim the front and back rather than the sides. Assemble the four 5-1/2" tall 1x6 pieces square, then glue and brad the 1/4" plywood bottom on — the bottom is what locks the box square, so check both diagonals as you nail it. Test the box in the opening and shave the sides if it binds.

ops.title: Build and attach the seat
ops.body: Build the seat as its own sub-assembly on the bench top before it goes on: the 11-1/4" x 36" 1x12 seat panel, the 36" 1x2 seat trim along the front edge, and the 36" 1x3 seat back trim at the rear. Join them with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws driven from underneath so the seat's top face stays clean. Sand the assembled seat and ease its front edge well — this is the edge people slide across. Then set the seat onto the bench and fasten from underneath, checking the overhang is even on both ends before the screws go in.

flags: SHORTFALL, 3/4" PLYWOOD — verified by area, which needs no packing assumption: the 3/4" plywood parts total about 16,630 square inches (side bench sides alone are 4,740 and the tops/bottoms another 4,562), while two 4x8 sheets supply 9,216. Even with perfect nesting that is at least four sheets, not two.

SHORTFALL, 1/4" PLYWOOD: the backs and drawer bottoms total about 6,136 square inches against one sheet's 4,608. Additionally the four 60" backs are each 15-1/2" wide, so no two share a 96" run — they need four separate 15-1/2" strips and a 48" sheet yields three.

SHORTFALL, PINE — verified with packBoards: the 1x2 parts (four 60" rails, one 76" rail, twenty-six 11-1/4" stiles, two 20-3/4" side trim) need 8 boards against 5 supplied. The 1x4 parts (four 60" cleats, one 76" rail, two 20-3/4" wide side trim) need 5 against 3 supplied.

Because the sheet and board counts are wrong across the board, the inserted cut step enumerates the parts and tells the builder to lay out and buy from the part list rather than from the materials quantities. It does NOT assert a corrected sheet count, since the right number depends on your nesting.
CORRECTNESS DEFECT: 'End bench sides' has quantity 1. A bench box needs two sides. The prose says two and flags the discrepancy rather than silently following the 1.

'Side bench face frame stiles' quantity is 18 across two benches with 6 dividers each — that is 9 per bench, consistent with 8 openings between dividers plus the ends. Not asserted beyond cutting the listed quantity.

scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan, so no claim is made about whether the 60" sides or the 28-7/8" tops/bottoms run the full outside length; the prose describes the joint without asserting which is captured.

step.title: Cut the parts — and check your sheet count first
step.body: Every plywood part in this bed is 19-3/4" deep, so break the sheets down by ripping 19-3/4" strips and crosscutting from those. Cut for the two side benches: four sides at 60", twelve dividers at 14-3/4", and eight tops/bottoms at 28-7/8". For the end bench: two sides at 76" (the cut list says one, but a box needs two), three dividers at 14-3/4", and two top/bottom at 36-7/8". From 1/4" plywood cut four side bench backs at 15-1/2" x 60", one end bench back at 15-1/2" x 76", and, if you are building drawers, two bottoms at 18" x 34-3/8". From 1x2 stock: four 60" rails, one 76" rail, twenty-six stiles at 11-1/4", and two side trim at 20-3/4". From 1x4: four back cleats at 60", one 76" trim rail, and two wide side trim at 20-3/4". Buy from this part list, not from the materials quantities — the two 3/4" sheets listed hold roughly 9,200 square inches while the parts above come to about 16,600, and the 1x2 and 1x4 counts fall similarly short. Lay every part out on paper against your real sheets before you buy or cut.

ops.title: Build the two side benches
ops.body: Each side bench is a plywood box 19-3/4" deep and 60" long with six fixed dividers inside it, making it a run of cubbies rather than one open box. Join every joint with 3/4" pocket holes and 1-1/4" pocket hole screws — three per joint is plenty in plywood — and glue as you go. Space the six dividers so the openings between them come out equal, laying out from the centre outward and using a cut scrap as a spacer so every opening matches, because a drawer built for one opening has to fit the next. Check both diagonals on each box before the last screws go in. Then screw a 60" 1x4 cleat along the back of each bench: those are what the mattress slats will rest on, so set them at the same height on both benches, measured the same way on each.

ops.title: Build the end bench
ops.body: The end bench is the same construction as the side benches and the only piece that changes between the queen and king versions — its 76" sides and 36-7/8" top and bottom are what span the king's extra width. Build it the same way: 3/4" pocket holes, 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, three per joint, glue everywhere, with the three 14-3/4" dividers evenly spaced and set with a scrap spacer. Note the cut list gives one 76" side where the box needs two — cut two. Check the diagonals before the glue sets, because this is the longest box in the build and the easiest one to rack.

ops.title: Assemble the three benches
ops.body: Carry the three finished benches into the room separately — that is the whole reason this bed is built as three boxes — and set them in a U with the end bench across the head and a side bench down each side. Screw the abutting corners together so the U cannot spread. Then cut scrap plywood to bridge the cavity between the benches and screw it down onto the mattress-slat cleats on the backs of the two side benches, which is what the mattress will actually rest on. Check the whole assembly is level and that the cleats on all three benches sit at one height before the mattress goes on.

baseStepTitles: Build and attach the face frames
Build and attach the end bench side trim

flags: SHORTFALL, 1x3 — verified with packBoards: the two 72" front side trim pieces each consume a whole 8 ft board and leave under 24", which cannot yield a 30" door frame piece; a third board holds only three of the four 30" pieces. That is 4 boards needed against 3 supplied. The door frame parts are marked 'sized to your actual door openings', so the exact figure may change — but the two 72" pieces alone tie up two boards regardless.

NOT a shortfall: the six 30-1/2" shelves against two 6 ft 1x12s looks short if you bin each shelf as a 30-1/2" rectangle (that would need three boards), but the shelves are trapezoids with both ends mitered at 45°, so alternating them nests each into the last one's offcut. The prose teaches the nesting instead of claiming a shortage.

GEOMETRY NOTE, derived arithmetic: mitering both ends of an 11-1/4" wide board at 45° makes the long edge exactly 22-1/2" longer than the short edge. So the cut list's 30-1/2" shelf corresponds to a back board right around 8", while the 'Back' row lists 7-1/4" and the description gives a 7" to 7-1/2" range. The prose states the 22-1/2" relationship so a builder can compute their own shelf length from their own board, which is what the source's self-adjusting method is for, and does not treat 30-1/2" as a number to cut blind.
scripts/run1-box-geometry.mjs returned no determination for this plan (there is no four-member rectangle), so nothing is asserted about captured members.
'Door frame stiles and rails' is a single row of 4 at 30", with the note that they are sized to the real openings — no stile-versus-rail split is given, so the prose does not assign lengths to them.
The 1x2 (3 ft) yields the 26-1/2" tabletop trim with room to spare; the single 1x4 (6 ft) yields both 26-1/2" top and bottom trim pieces. Both check out.

ops.title: Measure and cut the shelves
ops.body: Measure the actual width of your 1x8 back board first — it can run anywhere from 7" to 7-1/2" depending on the mill, and every shelf is sized off it. Cut one end of a 1x12 shelf blank at 45°, measure your real back-board width along the short edge, and make the second 45° cut perpendicular to the first. That gives a trapezoid whose long front edge runs exactly 22-1/2" longer than the back edge, because each 45° cut across an 11-1/4" board runs out 11-1/4"; the cut list's 30-1/2" corresponds to a back board right around 8", so compute your own figure rather than cutting to 30-1/2" blind. Repeat for all six shelves, and cut them alternating — flip the board over between shelves so each one nests into the offcut the last one left. Nesting is what makes two 6 ft 1x12s go far enough; cut them as separate rectangles and you will run out.

step.title: Cut the back and the trim
step.body: Cut the 1x8 back to 72", the same length as the two 1x3 front side trim pieces, so the cabinet's three vertical members all finish flush top and bottom. Cut those two front side trim pieces at 72" as well — note that each one uses a whole 8 ft 1x3 and leaves under 24", so the four 30" door frame pieces will not come out of the offcuts; buy a fourth 1x3 rather than discovering this at the doors step. Cut the top and bottom trim to 26-1/2" from the 1x4 and the tabletop trim to 26-1/2" from the 1x2, but treat all three as rough lengths and trim them to the assembled cabinet later, as the cut list's own notes say. Leave the beadboard, the crown moulding and the door parts uncut until their steps — every one of them is measured off the cabinet as built.

ops.title: Add the top and bottom trim
ops.body: Measure the front of the assembled cabinet rather than using the 26-1/2" from the cut list, since the finished front width depends on the back board you actually measured back in the first step. Cut the 1x4 top and bottom trim and the 1x2 tabletop trim to those measurements, dry-fit each before fastening, and attach with glue and 1-1/4" screws into the shelf edges and the front side trim behind them. Keep the top edge of the tabletop trim flush with the shelf it caps so the surface stays flat for anything set on it.

ops.title: Add the top moulding
ops.body: Crown on a corner cabinet meets at outside corners, so measure the top of the cabinet and cut the mitered returns from the 36" length of moulding — cut one, dry-fit it, then cut its partner, because a crown miter that is a degree off shows badly at eye level. Cut every piece long and creep up on the fit. Fasten with brad nails into the trim behind it and glue the miters themselves, which is what keeps the joint closed as the wood moves.

ops.title: Build the doors
ops.body: Measure your actual door opening first and size the 1x3 frame parts to it — the cut list's four 30" pieces are a starting length, not a finished one, and they will need a fourth 1x3 board since the two 72" trim pieces already took two. Build the overlay frame flat with a pocket hole jig, or with 3" fine screws through the joints, checking both diagonals as you clamp. Attach beadboard to the BACK of the assembled frame, sized to the opening in the shelf behind it. An inset door works too if you have a router or table saw to rabbet the frame, but keep the whole door under 3/4" thick — the shelf sits directly behind it and a thicker door will not close.

ops.title: Add the center post and hang the doors
ops.body: Fit a center post between the two doors from 1x3 offcut to close the gap where they meet and give the doors something to shut against. Hang each door on its pair of hinges, mortising the hinge leaves flush if you want the doors to sit tight, and adjust until the gap around and between them is even — hang the first door, get it right, then set the second to match it rather than to the cabinet. Sand every surface, fill any screw holes, and finish.

baseStepTitles: Attach the shelves to the back
Add the top and bottom trim
Add the center post and hang the doors
